edges.description: Loss of RSK2 dysregulates downstream ERK-dependent transcriptional control
Dysregulated ERK-Sp1 signaling increases downstream neuronal gene expression programs
Abnormal downstream signaling also perturbs musculoskeletal development
Altered neuronal signaling contributes to impaired cognitive and developmental trajectories
Altered neuronal excitability contributes to paroxysmal neurologic manifestations
edges.target: Seizure susceptibility and stimulus-induced drop episodes

nodes.description: RSK2 deficiency increases ERK-Sp1-dependent expression of the AMPA receptor subunit GluR2 in neuronal cells.

evidence.reference_title: Rsk2 Knockdown in PC12 Cells Results in Sp1 Dependent Increased Expression of the Gria2 Gene, Encoding the AMPA Receptor Subunit GluR2.
evidence.snippet: We show that Rsk2 silencing leads also to an elevation of ERK1/2 phosphorylation as well as of GluR2 expression and that the increased level of GluR2 expression results from the increased ERK1/2 activity on the transcription factor Sp1.

nodes.description: RSK2 normally modulates ERK output. RSK2 deficiency leads to abnormally increased ERK1/2 activity with altered transcriptional control in neural cells.

evidence.reference_title: Rsk2 Knockdown in PC12 Cells Results in Sp1 Dependent Increased Expression of the Gria2 Gene, Encoding the AMPA Receptor Subunit GluR2.
evidence.snippet: We show that Rsk2 silencing leads also to an elevation of ERK1/2 phosphorylation as well as of GluR2 expression and that the increased level of GluR2 expression results from the increased ERK1/2 activity on the transcription factor Sp1.

nodes.description: Downstream effects of RSK2 deficiency disrupt neurodevelopment, producing developmental delay and intellectual disability.

evidence.reference_title: Coffin-Lowry syndrome: a systematic review of RPS6KA3 confirmed cases and implications for diagnosis and counseling.
evidence.snippet: This systematic review highlights recurrent neurodevelopmental, neurologic, and skeletal phenotypes in CLS and delineates mutation-specific risks, particularly for SIDEs and seizures.

nodes.description: Downstream effects of RSK2 deficiency perturb skeletal development and are associated with progressive musculoskeletal deformity.

evidence.reference_title: Coffin-Lowry syndrome: a systematic review of RPS6KA3 confirmed cases and implications for diagnosis and counseling.
evidence.snippet: This systematic review highlights recurrent neurodevelopmental, neurologic, and skeletal phenotypes in CLS and delineates mutation-specific risks, particularly for SIDEs and seizures.

evidence.reference_title: Coffin-Lowry syndrome: a systematic review of RPS6KA3 confirmed cases and implications for diagnosis and counseling.
evidence.snippet: Coffin-Lowry syndrome (CLS) is a rare X-linked disorder caused by pathogenic variants in RPS6KA3, presenting with intellectual disability, distinctive facial and skeletal features, and variable systemic involvement.
RPS6KA3 | HGNC:10432 | Coffin-Lowry syndrome | MONDO:0010561 | XL | Definitive

nodes.description: Coffin-Lowry syndrome is driven by loss of function of the X-linked kinase RSK2 encoded by RPS6KA3.

evidence.reference_title: Rsk2 Knockdown in PC12 Cells Results in Sp1 Dependent Increased Expression of the Gria2 Gene, Encoding the AMPA Receptor Subunit GluR2.
evidence.snippet: Highly heterogeneous loss-of-function mutations affecting this gene are responsible for a severe syndromic form of cognitive impairment, Coffin-Lowry syndrome.

nodes.description: The neurologic phenotype includes recurrent seizures and stimulus-induced drop episodes in a clinically important subset of affected individuals.
nodes.id: Seizure susceptibility and stimulus-induced drop episodes

evidence.reference_title: Coffin-Lowry syndrome: a systematic review of RPS6KA3 confirmed cases and implications for diagnosis and counseling.
evidence.snippet: Developmental delay (87.5%) and intellectual disability (66.7%) were the most frequent features, alongside musculoskeletal deformities (kyphoscoliosis 33.3%, pectus anomalies 19.4%) and neurologic involvement (SIDEs 12.5%, seizures 15.3%, spasticity 5.6%).
